text: I usually have toast with scrambled eggs and a cuppa tea. Sometimes, if my husband was still alive, he'd join me for some bacon and sausages. Now that I'm living alone, I tend to stick to simple fare like cereal, yogurt, and fresh fruit. A bit of honey or jam is always nice on top. Nothing too fancy, really – just something comforting to start the day.

My usual lunch is a sandwich or leftovers from last night's dinner, really. Sometimes my grandkids will come round and we'll have something together, maybe some soup or a bit more substantial. If I'm out running errands, I might stop for a quick bite at the café near the market. Nothing fancy, mind – just whatever suits me best.
I'm a bit of an early bird, so I tend to be in bed by 10 o'clock most nights. It helps me feel refreshed and ready for the morning, which is why I try to get some sleep fairly early.

I think a bit of self-care is essential, even though some people might say we should be too busy for such luxuries! For me, getting enough sleep is really crucial – aiming for an early night and rising early allows me to feel refreshed and ready to tackle the day. A warm cuppa tea first thing in the morning helps set a gentle tone for the rest of my day, and before I know it, I'm sorted for a productive morning session of reading or tending to my garden. I try to take breaks throughout the day to stretch and move about; perhaps a quick walk round the block or some stretching exercises will refresh me. Afternoons tend to flag a bit after lunch, so I'll usually have something comforting like a slice of cake or a nice cuppa while I work. My evenings are generally spent relaxing with a good book or watching a documentary – nothing too taxing! It's funny how these little habits accumulate into what feels like a proper self-care routine, isn't it? It may not be grand or glamorous, but for someone like me who's had her share of ups and downs, taking care of myself has become quite vital.

I find networking is quite straightforward once you know who to talk to. In my former work as a GP surgery administrator, we had regular meetings between departments – usually every two weeks – where everyone could catch up and discuss any issues or concerns. It was an informal setting, which helped build rapport among colleagues. We'd chat about our personal lives too, so it wasn't all serious business! In terms of more structured networking opportunities, I attended departmental training sessions whenever there were relevant topics being covered. And I made sure to participate actively during those sessions, sharing thoughts and ideas rather than just sitting silently. If someone asked me a question or needed guidance, I was always happy to lend a hand. When a colleague moved away or left the organisation, we'd exchange contact information so we could keep in touch. Social gatherings like Christmas parties or summer barbecues provided another chance to connect informally outside of work-related contexts. Even though I'm fully retired now, some friendships formed while I worked have endured, making it easy to tap into them when I need advice or support.

I find a regular routine helps me stay focused while working from home. Since my peak productivity hours are usually between 8 am and 12 pm, I try to settle into work during those morning sessions. A cuppa tea always seems to give me an extra boost! Afternoons tend to be more relaxed, so I might take breaks to read something interesting or chat with the neighbour – nice way to recharge. If needed, I'll plan some quieter periods later in the afternoon to catch up on paperwork or respond to emails. Evening routines usually wind down nicely with some gentle stretching before dinner. It works out quite well for me, considering I'm still getting used to being retired!

I'm rather fussy about using video calls, to be honest! To me, there's something about seeing people face-to-face, even if we're just having a chat. But my grandsons have been encouraging me to make more use of them since Mum isn't here anymore. So, whenever we need to catch up, I'll put on my headphones and join a call, even though it takes some getting used to. It's lovely catching up with them like that, but sometimes I still prefer speaking on the phone – there's just something comforting about being able to hear each other's voices clearly.

I think it's all about setting a daily routine and sticking to it, really. As someone who was used to being in an office, my brain is still wired to work towards deadlines and tasks, so having some kind of framework helps me stay focused. Even though I'm retired now, I try to carve out specific blocks of time each day for different activities – like responding to emails, going for walks, or tending to my garden. It might sound simple, but having something to look forward to gives me a sense of purpose. Plus, having regular interactions with my grandkids and keeping busy with hobbies like baking or reading books keeps me feeling engaged. And, let's be honest, knowing I have plenty of free time does make it easier to avoid procrastination! Of course, there are days when motivation flags, but overall, having a mix of structure and flexibility seems to work for me.

I'm quite flexible, really. When something comes along that takes priority, like a big project deadline or an urgent matter involving patients, I adjust my plans accordingly. But I always try to make sure everything else gets done too – it's all part of being organised, isn't it? In my experience, setting realistic expectations helps prevent last-minute rushes. If someone knows what's expected of them, they're more likely to stay focused on what's truly important. And, of course, keeping a tidy workspace and having all relevant information readily available makes life so much easier! Nowadays, since retiring, I have more control over how I spend my time, which is lovely. It allows me to juggle multiple projects and hobbies simultaneously, should I choose to. Still, I believe prioritising your core objectives remains crucial; otherwise, one might feel like they're flitting between different topics rather than concentrating effectively!

I think advocating for myself has always been quite straightforward for me. As a former administrative assistant, I was very particular about getting my job done efficiently and effectively. If something wasn't going according to plan, I'd politely speak up and suggest an alternative solution rather than simply accepting whatever was proposed. My previous employers appreciated these concerns, which helped prevent unnecessary complications and streamlined our workflows. When dealing with colleagues or superiors, I'm honest yet considerate – never one to rock the boat unnecessarily. However, when faced with decisions affecting multiple people, I do stand my ground when necessary while still being open to compromise and negotiation. My motto is "patience and curiosity" gets you much farther than rushing through life; sometimes taking your time allows you to find better solutions all round!

When I feel like I'm being misunderstood, I try to take a step back, breathe deeply, and remind myself that everyone has different perspectives and opinions. I think about the situation and what might be causing any misunderstandings, whether it's a conversation, a letter, or an event. Sometimes I'll ask clarifying questions to make sure we're all on the same page, other times I'll give people space to express themselves and let them share their thoughts. If necessary, I'll politely explain my point of view again, using simple language so there's no confusion. It's essential for me to remember that even though I strive to communicate clearly, others may still misinterpret or forget what was said. My husband always used to say that I should repeat everything twice – once slowly and loudly! Now he'd probably tell me to relax, that most people will eventually catch up, which helps put some perspective into my daily interactions.

I love spending time with my grandkids! During family gatherings, we usually have a lovely time together. My granddaughter is always eager to show me her latest projects, whether it's drawing something beautiful or playing some music. We'll sit down, chat, and have a nice cuppa together while watching them play. Occasionally, my son will bring his kids along, so there's quite a lively atmosphere! We might have a big Sunday roast dinner or bake some treats together – the kids adore my famous scones and cakes. After lunch, we'll take a walk around the block or visit our local park nearby. Sometimes, one of us will be engrossed in a game or puzzle, like a jigsaw or crosswords. It's really wonderful to catch up and make memories with all those little faces around me. It's lovely being surrounded by such a loving family; I feel truly blessed.

To be honest, I'm a bit wary of these newer forms of networking. As someone who has worked mainly in traditional industries like education and healthcare, my comfort zone is more in face-to-face interactions rather than digital ones. However, since some colleagues still seem to find value in having an online presence, I suppose I'd consider joining something similar to LinkedIn if it was made easy enough. But only if it serves a purpose and isn't too much fuss. Perhaps if there were tutorials available to guide me along the way!

I think sensitivity is key when discussing delicate subjects with others. When approaching a conversation where someone might be upset or vulnerable, I try to start with an empathetic ear rather than jumping straight into specifics. A gentle tone and body language go a long way in setting the right atmosphere. It's essential to show genuine interest in how they're feeling before delving deeper. Sometimes, simply allowing them space to express themselves without interruption helps me gauge what we need to discuss. Of course, there will always be certain conversations where tact must prevail – being honest yet compassionate balances nicely.

Well, I'm a bit of an ambivert, so I value my relationships and like being part of groups, but I also appreciate some me-time and space to think things through. When faced with peer pressure, I try to weigh the pros and cons and consider what's best for myself rather than just going along with the crowd. If everyone else is pushing something, I'll usually take a moment to reflect on why I feel uncomfortable or unsure. Sometimes, having a chat with someone close to me helps clarify things – whether it's a trusted partner, child, or grandchild. They might offer a different perspective or remind me of our shared priorities, which can make all the difference. Ultimately, I'd rather be honest with others and say "no" or "not now" if needed, even if it makes them uncomfortable; I figure being true to oneself is more important than pleasing everyone else.

I try to limit my exposure to them whenever possible. To be honest, I find all those personal messages and recommendations based on what I supposedly like or buy a bit unsettling. Sometimes I'll mute certain websites or turn off location services on my device so they're not tracking me around. But sometimes I'm forced into seeing them because we still have some subscriptions or purchases linked to our account. My grandkids always laugh at how annoyed I am by them! They say "Grandma, why are you getting upset?" And I tell them, "It's just these blasted advertisers!"

To be honest, I find it a bit overwhelming. There are so many windows open all the time – emails, messages, my granddaughter wants me to check her latest drawings... It helps having them organised into folders, I suppose, but sometimes even that gets jumbled. My grandchild has been teaching me how to set reminders on her phone and using those little icons to categorise everything, which is quite clever. But really, most of the time, I stick to what feels like an orderly system: one window at a time. Simple, straightforward. If something urgent comes along, I'll close other tabs and deal with it straight away.

I try to ignore them, really. As a rule, I find most people aren't worth getting worked up over. If someone makes a comment that's intentionally hurtful or upsetting, I might have a quick word with them privately – usually my son does it more effectively than me, he knows how to handle those sorts of conversations! But generally speaking, I believe that engaging with negativity only gives them what they're looking for; it lets them know we're still bothered by their words. Instead, I focus on being kind and courteous, even if others choose to be unpleasant. It's all part of staying level-headed, isn't it?

I always try to remain calm and explain the situation clearly. If my son is available, he usually steps in and helps me troubleshoot over the phone while we're still talking, so someone else can assist remotely. He knows how much I dislike getting frustrated with these sorts of issues, so he tries his best to walk me through what might be wrong and find a solution together.

I'm quite methodical, really. As soon as my bank statements come out, I sort them into three piles: essential bills like council tax and utility payments, those which can be spread across multiple months like mortgage and insurance premiums, and any smaller ones such as subscriptions or credit cards. Then, I make a list and work on prioritising who needs payment first. My grandkids always say I should set reminders, so whenever we have our regular chats on WhatsApp, I let them know what bill is due and ask them to add it to their calendars too – helps me stay on track! Once everything has been scheduled, I sit down at my desk with all the paperwork, check each item one last time, fill in the necessary amounts, date, and signature...and then send it all off via post. It might seem old-fashioned, but there's something satisfying about getting it done properly before the deadline. Plus, being organised gives me peace of mind, knowing everythin' is taken care of until the next statement arrives.

Well, I think it's a case-by-case thing really. You have to weigh what you're getting out of each subscription against how much money it costs me. If something is just taking up space or collecting dust, chances are it's worth cutting ties. On the other hand, if there's some sort of value being delivered – be it entertainment, education, or convenience – it might be worth keeping. And usually, my grandkids give me an honest opinion too; they know exactly what's worthwhile! We sit down together and go through them one by one. It makes it more pleasant and helps us make better decisions, all round.

I'm a bit old-fashioned, I suppose, but before making any major purchase, I like to sit down and think it through really thoroughly. I make a list of all my options, consider what I'll need, how much I have available, and whether there's more value elsewhere. It might be months or even longer between me buying something significant – I prefer to wait until I know exactly where I am financially and what works best for me. My late husband always said we should never buy just because someone was pressuring us into it; he'd say, "Wait until the penny pincher is satisfied." Still, I must admit, sometimes our son will tease me about being too frugal!

I think I'm a bit of an old-fashioned soul, even though my grandkids might say otherwise! For me, when deciding whether to buy something new or second-hand, I always ask myself two main questions. Firstly, is it essential, does it bring significant value into my life, or will it solve a pressing need? If so, I'd lean towards buying new – there's usually peace of mind knowing exactly what I'm getting. On the other hand, if it's more of a luxury item or someone has kindly offered it to me, and it'll be put to good use before needing replacement soon enough... then I consider going second-hand. It's amazing how much character you can find in pre-loved items, isn't it? My late husband was quite handy too; he could fix nearly everything himself. But now, since his passing, I try to weigh factors like price, maintenance costs, and sentimental value when making decisions. And let me tell you, I've become rather adept at haggling during car boots or browsing charity shops! Ultimately, I suppose I follow the saying "you can't take it with you," which makes me appreciate each purchase all the more.

I think it's a matter of being mindful of one's spending habits, really. You know, my mother used to say "waste not, want not" all the time, and that stuck with me, even now. We didn't have much money growing up, so we had to make every penny count. When I was younger, I took a very strict approach to managing our finances – always looking out for bargains, cooking meals from scratch instead of eating out, buying second-hand whenever possible... those sorts of things. Nowadays, I still try to be prudent. If I need something, I ask myself if I absolutely must have it. And I stick to my budget, keeping track of everything and making sure I'm staying within reason. Of course, there will inevitably be exceptions – like birthdays and Christmas – but overall, I believe living below your means is essential for avoiding debt. It's not worth putting yourself into a situation where you're struggling to pay bills because of overspending. Plus, having some savings set aside gives you peace of mind, knowing you'll be okay come what may.

I love keeping an eye out for those seasonal sales! You know, my grandkids always say I'm like a hawk waiting for discounts, ready to pounce on any bargain. I try to plan ahead, making sure to check our local newspapers and catalogues before they go to press, so we're aware of what's coming. We usually stick to essentials – food, toiletries, and maybe some nice gifts for the kids. My husband used to be quite handy, he'd fix stuff himself, but since his passing...well, I take care of most DIY jobs now. Still, I find myself looking forward to decorating the living room every autumn, preparing everything for Christmas, even though it feels more subdued these days without him.

I believe moderation is key, really. Growing up, my mother always said "use what you need, waste not." It's stuck with me all these years. When cooking for myself or making meals for others, I try to make sure there's enough food, but not so much that we'll end up throwing some away. My grandkids love coming over, and I like being able to share a meal together, but I'm mindful not to overdo it. After all, there's always tomorrow, isn't there? And besides, too many sweets or treats will undo all the hard work going into cooking something wholesome. Simple habits like that have helped me develop a bit of discipline – helps me avoid excess altogether.

I think a gentle, human-to-human conversation is key. None of those fancy talking heads or scripted sessions for me, thank you very much! Just someone who listens attentively, asks insightful questions, and offers guidance based on real-life experience. A bit like my late husband used to be before he...well, you know. He had a way of putting into words what I was feeling, even though we didn't always agree on everything. Now, I suppose I'm looking forward to having some company while figuring out how to navigate these changes in my later years. But I must say, I value being able to share a cuppa and have a proper chat rather than sitting across from someone telling me what to do all the time.

I think prevention is key, isn't it? For me, taking breaks throughout the day helps a lot. Even little ones – like going for a quick walk outside during my morning tea break – make a big difference. And, of course, having something to look forward to, whether it's a cuppa with some nice biscuits or spending time with loved ones, gives me a boost too. As someone who's had her share of busy periods in work, managing workload has always been essential to avoiding burnout. Breaking tasks into manageable chunks and prioritising what's really important have helped me cope better. Learning to say "no" when needed and setting realistic expectations for myself hasn't hurt either! Getting enough restful sleep also plays its part; getting ready for bed early ensures I'm feeling refreshed come morning. A bit of exercise each day never hurts either – nothing too strenuous, mind! It sounds daft, but keeping a sense of humour has gotten me out of more scrapes than I can count. Laughing away worries makes them seem smaller, and looking at the bigger picture reminds me there's more to life than whatever's stressing me now. Oh, and making sure I take some time for hobbies – gardening, reading... those things give me space to

I find a regular bedtime routine really helps me wind down before sleeping. It starts with getting my reading done during my peak productivity hours – usually between 7 and 9 pm. Then, I take some time to tidy up the living room and kitchen, making sure everything is in its place for tomorrow morning. After that, I make myself a nice cup of herbal tea, sit comfortably in my favourite armchair, and spend some time journaling or simply reflecting on my day. The soft glow of the bedside lamp always makes me feel cozy. Once I'm feeling relaxed, I climb into bed, set an alarm for 6:30 am, and try to be out cold before midnight. Sometimes, I might listen to calming music or nature sounds, which seem to lull me into a peaceful slumber. If I'm having trouble falling asleep, I remind myself that it's okay and give myself permission to rest. A bit of gentle stretching or deep breathing exercises can work wonders too! It may sound simple, but consistency seems to be key for me. And, of course, sticking to a reasonable amount of screen time before bed never hurts either!

I like to mix it up a bit, really. You know, keep my body guessing so it stays interested. On weekdays, I usually start with some gentle stretching and yoga in the morning before getting dressed. Then, I'll take a brisk walk along the canal nearby – fresh air does me good! If the weather permits, I might squeeze in some tending to the vegetable patch out front; there's something satisfying about watching those seeds sprout into actual plants, isn't there? On Saturdays, I love taking a longer stroll through the countryside, maybe stopping for a cuppa and a sit-down break halfway round. Sundays, though...that's usually spent pottering about the kitchen, perhaps making a big Sunday roast or whipping up a batch of homemade scones for the grandkids who come visit. They always make an effort to spend more time together during the weekend, which is lovely. Occasionally, we'll plan a little outing – a museum visit or a drive to somewhere scenic – where I can indulge my sense of adventure and soak up all sorts of interesting bits and pieces. Oh, and have I mentioned our local park has some cracking trails suitable for walking? Those are perfect for clearing my head and unwinding after a busy week. It's never too

I'd say I'm a very methodical thinker, always try to consider all sides before making a decision. I like to weigh up the pros and cons, think about what might happen each way, and ask myself questions like "what's the worst that could go wrong?" It's quite simple really – just breaking it down step by step, taking my time, and trusting my instincts usually does me fine. Of course, sometimes other people have helped me out too, offering advice or pointing out things I hadn't thought of. But overall, I find that having some space to think makes sense to me.

I think incorporating feedback is a vital part of making any improvement or adjustment work effectively. When receiving constructive comments or suggestions, I like to take my time to consider them thoughtfully before responding. It helps me process what was said, weigh the pros and cons, and figure out whether there's validity to the point being made. If someone has taken the trouble to share their thoughts, I appreciate their input and try to be receptive, even if we might have differing opinions. Sometimes, simply acknowledging their perspective can go a long way – a simple "Thank you for sharing your thoughts" or "That makes an interesting point" shows I'm actively listening and value their contribution. However, I must admit I tend towards needing concrete examples and evidence-based reasoning rather than vague criticisms. If all anyone says is something feels wrong or isn't quite right, it's hard for me to know where to begin fixing it! A bit more specific guidance would really help me make sense of things. Ultimately, once I feel I grasp the essence of the issue, I'll usually look for opportunities to implement changes gradually. Big transformations may require some persuasion and motivation, so breaking things down into manageable steps seems helpful. Perhaps starting with tiny adjustments here and there will allow us to build momentum while avoiding feeling overwhelmed

I think my approach to analytical thinking is quite straightforward, really. I like to start with a clear objective in mind, whether it's solving a problem or making a decision. Then, I try to break it down into smaller parts, identifying all the relevant factors and information involved. It's rather like untangling a knot – once you know what threads make it up, you can work out how to fix it! Once I have everything laid out before me, I prefer to look at different aspects one by one, considering each point thoroughly. Sometimes, taking notes helps, so I can refer back to them later. I'm not too keen on brainstorming sessions where everyone talks all at once; I find it difficult to follow conversations when there are too many ideas flying about! Instead, I tend to think best when I can focus on one thing at a time. Now, I am aware I might be more inclined towards a logical, step-by-step approach. If someone presents an argument, I'll consider the evidence presented and weigh up the pros and cons. However, I will admit to sometimes getting bogged down in details – a bit like getting lost in a dense thicket of brambles! But generally speaking, once I've identified the key issues, I should be

I find that writing things down helps me quite a bit. If something is unclear or I'm struggling to remember what someone said, I'll jot down notes afterwards so I can refer back to them later. It's like having my own little memory aid, really. Sometimes I'll even talk through things out loud while I'm writing it down – that seems to make sense of it all more clearly. And, of course, revisiting things the following day usually gives everything into perspective. As an example, we were discussing plans for our granddaughter's birthday last night... now that morning, I found myself wondering which recipe we should have for cake... after checking some old notebooks, I remembered exactly what Mum used to make! So, yes, getting things down in writing does seem to be very helpful for keeping track of information.

I think I identify areas for growth quite simply - where my mind starts racing with thoughts like "there must be a better way" or "I wish I could..." Usually it comes from reflecting on how something didn't go smoothly, whether it was a difficult conversation with someone, struggling to manage my workload, or an issue I'm still figuring out how to tackle. Sometimes it'll be a piece of information I come across while researching or watching educational programmes that sparks a desire to know more, perhaps sparking a hobby interest or wanting to take on a new project. My grandkids always say I need to update me tech skills, so there might be bits of that too! Overall, identifying areas for growth is usually pretty organic – one thing nags away until I feel compelled to look into what I can improve upon.

I'd say my strategy is a bit old-fashioned, but effective nonetheless. For me, it's all about being open to it initially, yes. You know, making an effort to be curious and considerate of what could come out of it. And once I start getting into something, I think having someone to guide me along the way helps. It might be a grandchild explaining how this fancy gadget works or a colleague showing me some technique. Having that support makes it feel more manageable. Of course, there are moments where I still struggle – perhaps because I'm a bit set in my ways – but overall, I suppose I try to take it one step at a time and focus on finding benefits rather than worrying about difficulties.

I always make sure to carve out some time each day, even if it's just a little bit. Since my morning is usually quite busy getting ready for the day, I like to have a cuppa while I'm sitting here having breakfast before starting any work or other obligations. It gives me a chance to wind down and catch up on something else. As for how much time, I'd say it varies – sometimes I might finish a chapter or two during a break between meetings or chores, and other times I'll spend an hour or more curled up with a good book after dinner. Whatever fits into the schedule really! And of course, spending time with the grandkids helps too; we love snuggling up together with books and sharing adventures.

I usually find out about new authors or genres through word-of-mouth recommendations from my family members, friends, or colleagues. They'll tell me about a good book they read and think I'd enjoy, so I take note of them and add them to my list. Sometimes, I stumble upon something interesting while browsing through libraries or second-hand bookshops – those places always seem to have some hidden gems. Occasionally, I might pick up a magazine related to history, nature, or cooking and come across an author who catches my eye. And, of course, there's the local book clubs we attend; people share what they're currently reading and discussing, which is great way to broaden one's horizons.

I find myself tidying away any clutter or paperwork before settling into a bit of gentle cleaning while humming along to my favourite tunes. It helps me unwind and gets everything done more efficiently, which is perfect for an organised soul like mine!

I find I rather enjoy listening to podcasts and audiobooks while doing other things like tidying up my garden or cooking. It helps me relax and fills the time quite nicely. I prefer a gentle pace though, so I tend to stick with longer episodes or books rather than shorter ones. And yes, I must admit, I still prefer having something tangible, like paper pages or a physical copy – there's something comforting about holding those, even if it is more convenient to listen now. Still, audiobooks have grown on me; perhaps one day I'll figure out how to manage them properly!

I love staying active! Since my husband has been gone, I try to make sure I'm keeping myself busy and enjoying some hobbies. My absolute favourite thing is tending to my little vegetable garden out back – there's something so satisfying watching everything grow from seeds into a lovely salad. It takes me a bit of effort, but it's wonderful exercise too. Plus, I get fresh produce straight away! In addition to gardening, I also like taking long walks along our local park or strolling round the village. There aren't many hills, which suits me fine since I have arthritis, but still gets the blood flowing and clears my head. And sometimes I'll pop into the nearby nature reserve to watch the birds and take in the views – really peaceful. When the weather permits, I might join forces with some neighbours for a game of bowls or we'll attend one of those nice outdoor concerts at the village hall. Nothing fancy, just lots of laughter and camaraderie, usually followed by a nice cuppa afterwards. Those gatherings always lift my spirits. Occasionally, I'll treat myself to an afternoon at the local theatre or cinema, catching a film or play. Even though I'm getting older, I find it helps keep me feeling young and entertained! What more could

I thoroughly enjoy my alone time, actually. There's something so peaceful about having some space to myself where I can think, read a book, tend to my garden... You know, recharge my batteries. As an introvert, I find I'm more productive when I have some time to focus on one thing before interacting with others. And I must say, I rather appreciate being able to come home from a busy morning out and simply put my feet up, make myself a cuppa, and watch a bit of telly or listen to music. It's lovely to be able to unwind like that. Of course, with the grandkids coming to visit sometimes, it's hard to resist all their energy! But overall, I cherish my downtime – it helps me feel grounded and ready to tackle whatever comes next.

I must say, I'm quite adventurous when it comes to cooking, although I wouldn't call myself a master chef! When trying new cuisines, I usually start by looking up simple recipes online, maybe checking out cookbooks from my local library or browsing food blogs. My grandkids always seem to find some lovely international dishes to inspire me. If we're having dinner together, I'll ask them what type of cuisine they'd like, and we might try something different each month. We love exploring different cultures through our food, whether it's Italian pasta, Indian curries, Chinese stir-fries... You know, keeping things interesting is key! Nowadays, since I live alone most of the time, I tend to stick to tried-and-tested favourites – perhaps a hearty beef stew or roast chicken. But every now and then, I treat myself to a nice meal kit from Waitrose or Ocado; those make wonderful changes to the usual suspects! Overall, I believe there's so much delight to discover in the world of flavours, and I thoroughly enjoy sharing meals with loved ones while experiencing all sorts of culinary adventures.

I think one way I achieve variety is by balancing different hobbies and activities. For instance, while my grandkids love coming to visit me here, we usually have a nice cuppa together and bake something tasty like scones or cookies. Then there's my little vegetable garden out back – tending to those plants really helps me unwind, and I'm always discovering what's blooming. Occasionally, I'll join local groups – our village has a lovely community centre where people meet to discuss books, watch films, or participate in various projects. It's great to be part of such friendly gatherings. Sometimes, though, it feels wonderful simply sitting with a good book and a warm blanket; getting lost in some engaging history novel is pure bliss!

I like keeping a little notebook where I jot down my plans for the weekend or any upcoming outings. It helps me remember what I need to pick up at the shop or who I'm meeting up with. And sometimes I'll include some notes about how things went afterwards – whether someone made us laugh or had an interesting story to share. I find it makes looking back easy too, so I can relive those moments again whenever I'd like.

I'm quite particular about keeping my belongings tidy and making sure everything has its place. It's a habit developed over the years, really. For me, having an organised system helps reduce stress and makes daily routines much smoother. My mainstay is still using a planner – actually two separate ones, one for personal stuff like appointments, bills, and medication reminders, and another for work-related tasks and projects. The calendar aspect keeps all my various obligations and deadlines straight. When it comes to paperwork and documents, I tend to file them away into labeled folders on shelves here in the living room. My computer files have labels too; although I must admit, I find digital decluttering rather daunting – sometimes I ask my grandchild to sort out those for me! In any case, knowing where every piece of paper is does give me peace of mind. Every morning starts with tidying up – putting dishes away from last night's dinner, laying out clothes ready for the day ahead, setting out breakfast... You know how some people say we're more productive first thing? It works for me because getting started feels so satisfying! Nowadays, since retiring, there isn't such urgency, yet finding ways to fill the extra free time remains interesting. Occasionally taking up crafting hobbies,

Before I set out, I like to make myself a nice cuppa tea, perhaps some toast with marmalade or jam while catching up on the news on my radio. Then I take a quick glance at my calendar to check what's scheduled for the day – maybe I have an appointment or need to run some errands. After that, I slip into something comfy – usually one of those soft blouses I love – grab my bag, and I'm ready to go. If I've got any gardening to attend to beforehand, I might squeeze in a bit of weeding or watering before heading out. It sets me up nicely for a productive morning, even if only for a little while!

I try my best to be mindful of using only what we need, turning lights off when leaving a room, and closing curtains during winter months to warm our home properly. We have an older boiler, so we make sure to bleed air into radiators regularly, which helps circulation. In terms of insulation, we're lucky enough to still live in a relatively modern property, although I'm aware some houses could do with more work. Since moving here, I've planted quite a number of trees and shrubs outside to provide shade and reduce summer temperatures inside. Overall, it's all about making little changes that add up – nothing too drastic, really!

To be honest, I'm still a bit wary of these new-fangled contraptions. I mean, what's wrong with a simple lamp switch or a radio dial, eh? But my granddaughter has been going on about how much she loves her Alexa thingy, so I suppose I'll have to give one a go. The trouble is, I find all those buttons and screens overwhelming – I'd rather stick to something more traditional, like turning lights on with a switch. Still, I know some people love them, so perhaps I'll try to adapt. Just need someone to show me the ropes first!

I think prioritising is quite simple, really. You have to know what's truly important to you and stick to it, even when there are lots going on. For me, staying healthy and being able to spend quality time with my grandkids come top. After that, keeping a tidy and organised home and enjoying some hobbies like gardening take priority too. It's all about finding balance, isn't it? When faced with conflicting priorities, I ask myself which one will bring more joy or satisfaction in the long run. If something's causing me undue stress or anxiety, I'll reassess whether it's worth continuing to push forward. And remember, saying "no" is okay – sometimes letting go of lesser priorities allows space for greater ones to flourish.
